content.text: What Causes Vanishing and Exploding Gradients
Vanishing and exploding gradients are mainly caused by
how gradients are repeatedly multiplied during backpropagation

, especially in deep networks and recurrent networks. When those repeated multipliers are mostly smaller than 1, gradients shrink toward zero; when they are mostly larger than 1, gradients grow uncontrollably.

can cause gradients to vanish because their derivatives become very small in saturated regions. If a neuron is pushed into the flat part of the curve, backpropagation gets a tiny gradient and earlier layers learn very slowly or not at all.

The deeper the network, the more times the gradient has to pass backward through layers, so small changes get multiplied many times. This repeated multiplication is what makes the problem grow exponentially with depth.
Recurrent neural networks are especially vulnerable because the
same weights are reused across many time steps
, so the gradient is multiplied repeatedly through time as well as through layers. That is why long sequence modeling is so prone to vanishing or exploding gradients.
, activations and gradients tend to shrink as they propagate, which encourages vanishing gradients. If weights are initialized
, they can amplify signals and produce exploding gradients.

A learning rate that is too large can make parameter updates jump too far, which can destabilize training and contribute to exploding gradients. A very small learning rate can also make learning appear stalled, which can worsen the practical effect of vanishing gradients.

Inputs with large or uneven scales can push activations into unstable ranges, which makes saturation and gradient instability more likely. This is why normalization and proper preprocessing often help.
Gradients become very small, close to zero
Saturating activations, too-deep chains, small weights
Gradients become very large and unstable
Large weights, deep recurrence, high learning rates

Backpropagation uses the chain rule, so each layer contributes a multiplier to the gradient. If the average multiplier is below 1, the product shrinks exponentially; if it is above 1, the product grows exponentially. That is the core reason these problems become severe in deep and recurrent architectures.

In generative AI, these issues matter because sequence models need to preserve information over many steps. If gradients vanish, earlier tokens or layers stop learning useful long-range structure; if they explode, training can become unstable or diverge.

Think of backpropagation like sending a message through a long line of people. If each person whispers a little quieter than the last, the message disappears; if each person repeats it louder, it becomes chaotic. Vanishing gradients are the whispering problem, and exploding gradients are the shouting problem.
Vanishing and exploding gradients come from
often cause vanishing gradients because their derivatives saturate near zero.
make the problem worse because the gradient must pass through many layers.
are especially vulnerable because the same weights are reused across many time steps.
can shrink or amplify gradients before training even starts.
can contribute to exploding gradients and unstable training.
